original_abstract: As deep learning models continue to achieve state-of-the-art results across domains such as computer vision and natural language processing, their increasing complexity has led to a dramatic rise in parameter counts, computational latency, and training resource requirements. Consequently, optimizing the efficiency of these models has become as critical as improving their predictive quality. This paper provides a comprehensive survey of the efficient deep learning landscape, spanning five core pillars: model compression, learning techniques, automation, efficient architectures, and infrastructure (including hardware and software tooling). We provide a systematic overview of seminal research in areas such as quantization, pruning, distillation, and neural architecture search. Beyond a literature review, we introduce a mental model for practitioners to navigate the trade-offs between model quality and footprint through strategies like "shrink-and-improve." We supplement this framework with an experiment-based guide and open-source code, demonstrating how these techniques can be combined to achieve significant gains in model size and latency on mobile devices. To our knowledge, this is the first survey to offer a holistic view of efficiency from high-level modeling down to hardware-specific support, equipping both researchers and practitioners with the tools to build faster, smaller, and more sustainable deep learning systems.
humanized_abstract: High-performance deep learning (DLC) models are constantly producing state-of-the-art results in computer vision and natural language processing (NLP) and many other applications. With the progress in high-performance computing and advanced training resources, high-performance DLC models are usually of high complexity, which leads to huge model parameters, long inference latency, and large training resources. As a result, model efficiency is as important as the model performance. In this survey, we investigate and summarize the efficient deep learning techniques along five aspects, i.e., model compression, learning, automation, network architecture design, and infrastructure (including hardware and software). Also, we give a full picture of the fundamental works in quantization, pruning, knowledge distillation and neural architecture search (NAS). A mental model is proposed to assist practitioners to decide which efficient deep learning approaches should be applied to their own models at which stage of their development, namely at "shrink-and-improve." The latter is supported by an experiment guide with corresponding open-source code. To the best of our knowledge, our work is the first survey that explores the efficient deep insights into the infrastructure for model efficiency, spanning all the way from the modeling aspects down to the hardware supports, which would largely facilitate the development of the future efficient deep learning systems for researchers and practitioners.
